Name used for several processes by which words, pictures, or designs are reproduced on paper, fabrics, metal, or other suitable materials.
In physics and engineering, the property of a body that causes it to return to its original position or motion once it has been disturbed from a state of equilibrium or steady motion.
Petroleum field located in northeastern China in Heilongjiang Province, the source of much of China's petroleum and natural gas.
Pakistani physicist and Nobel laureate known for his research of the electromagnetic and weak interactions between atomic particles.
In physics, transfer of energy from one part of a substance to another, or from one body to another by virtue of a difference in temperature.
Lighter-than-air craft equipped with a bag containing a gas to lift the ship, a means of propulsion, and a means for adjusting buoyancy.
Preeminent Greek mathematician and inventor who wrote important works on geometry, arithmetic, and mechanics.
Colorless, odorless gas of the alkane series of hydrocarbons, formula C3H8, that occurs in crude oil and natural gas and as a by-product of petroleum refining.
Mineral consisting of hydrated silica (SiO2.NH2O); it has been used as a gemstone for many centuries in spite of a superstition that the gem brings bad luck to its owners.
Mineral consisting of beryllium aluminate (BeAl2O4); one variety, alexandrite, can be very valuable as a gemstone.
Russian-born American broadcasting executive who sponsored many innovations in the field of radio and television.
American chemist and Nobel laureate known for his discovery of new chemical elements and many radioactive isotopes.
Liquid with a chloroform odor, used in low concentrations as an additive in leaded gasolines to reduce formation of lead deposits.
German-born American physicist and Nobel laureate, best known as the creator of the theory of relativity.
Homogeneous portion of matter that has a definite, orderly atomic structure, and an outward form bounded by smooth, plane surfaces, symmetrically arranged.
Two-level suspension toll bridge for vehicular traffic that spans the Hudson River between New York City and Fort Lee, New Jersey.
German meteorologist who advocated the theory of continental drift at a time when the technological means for proving the theory had not yet been developed.
German chemist and Nobel laureate who specialized in diene synthesis, which is the analysis and formation of complex organic compounds.
Luminous atmospheric phenomenon occurring most frequently above 60 North or South latitude.
American inventor and engineer who designed the first efficient steamboat, thus inaugurating a new era of power-driven navigation.
Genus of swift, ostrich-size dinosaurs of the suborder Theropoda, which lived about 75 million years ago during the Late Cretaceous period.
A duplicate copy of a computer program, disk, or data, made for archiving purposes or to safeguard valuable files from loss.
German-born British astronomer who was the first important woman astronomer; she is known for her discovery of eight comets and three nebulas.
In geology, a rounded, symmetrical formation, occurring particularly in fine grained sediments such as chalk, limestone, and clay.
High-speed military attack craft used by the United States Navy until 1968; it is armed with two to four torpedoes, 20-mm and 40-mm guns, and several depth charges.
Fourth division of the Paleozoic Era of the geologic time scale, spanning an interval from about 408 million to 360 million years ago.
Dutch-born American astronomer noted for his studies of the solar system and for his work with the United States space program.
The apparent displacement of the position of a celestial object on the celestial sphere when viewed from two different positions.
This geological feature begins as a troughlike belt of sediment that eventually becomes severely folded and uplifted to form a mountain chain.
American inventor who discovered the process of vulcanization, still the basis of the rubber-manufacturing industry in the United States.
American astronomer who is best known for compiling a bibliography of about 200,000 variable stars and for completing a catalog of more than 350,000 stellar spectra.
British inventor and industrialist who is credited with inventing the spinning jenny in 1764, which made possible the automatic production of cotton thread.
British chemist who isolated and described several gases, including oxygen, and who is considered one of the founders of modern chemistry.
Prized throughout the world as a gemstone, this mineral's color ranges from blue and blue-green to greenish-gray according to the various amounts of copper present.
Branch of mathematics that deals with determining quantitatively the likelihood that an event or experiment will have a particular outcome.
Electrostatic dry-printing process for the reproduction of images or documents, widely employed in commerce and industry in copying machines.
Series of six chemical elements in group 1 (or Ia) of the periodic table; they are so reactive that they are never found in nature uncombined with other elements.
An important astronomical instrument that consists of a telescope fixed to a horizontal axis, so as to revolve in the plane of the meridian.
An evacuated glass container, having at one end a negative electrode, and a device called an electron gun that projects a beam of electrons.
In physical science, the flow of energy or matter from a higher concentration to a lower concentration, resulting in a homogeneous distribution.
Artificial waterway constructed for purposes of irrigation, drainage, or navigation, or in connection with a hydroelectric dam.
System of manufacture designed to extend the capacity of machines to perform certain tasks formerly done by humans, and to control sequences of operations without human intervention.
Austrian physicist and Nobel laureate who is best known for his mathematical studies of the wave mechanics of orbiting electrons.
Mineral regarded as an iron titanate (FeTiO3), the chief source of titanium dioxide, which is used in paint pigments and welding-rod coatings.
Crystalline, compact variety of metamorphosed limestone consisting primarily of calcite, dolomite, or a combination of both minerals.
In chemistry, law stating that many of the physical and chemical properties of the elements tend to recur in a systematic manner with increasing atomic number.
Large group of aluminosilicate minerals that form an important constituent of many igneous and metamorphic rocks, including granite, gneiss, and basalt,
American physicist who described the processes of nuclear synthesis of chemical elements within stars.
British physicist, engineer, and Nobel laureate known for his  development of holography, a lensless system of three-dimensional photography.
United States radio astronomy facility with headquarters and several large instruments at Green Bank, West Virginia.
American theoretical physicist and Nobel laureate who is best known for his studies of the nature and behavior of elementary particles.
In physics, chemistry, and engineering, a repeated motion back and forth past a central neutral position or position of equilibrium.
Field in which celestial objects and astrophysical phenomena are studied by examining their emission of electromagnetic radiation in the radio portion of the spectrum.
In computer science, generally, the time required for information to be gathered from some remote source, such as data from a computer's memory or data from a hard disk.
In thermodynamics, any physical process in which quantities such as pressure and volume are varied without any essential transfer of heat energy to the surroundings.
British mathematician and inventor who designed and built mechanical computing machines that anticipated the modern electronic computer.

Italian physician and mathematician who was the first to attempt to demonstrate the impossibility of solving general equations of any degree higher than the fourth by the elementary process of algebra.
Russian mathematician who was one of the first to apply a critical treatment to the fundamental postulates of Euclidean geometry.
British physicist who received the Nobel prize in physics in 1947 for his discovery of the layer of the ionosphere that reflects high-frequency radio waves.
In electronics, common name for a group of electronic devices used as amplifiers or oscillators in communications, control, and computer systems.
Important class of organic compounds that contain both the amino (-NH2) and carboxyl (-COOH) groups and serve as the building blocks of proteins.
American applied mathematician and electrical engineer who is noted for his development of the theory of communication now known as information theory.
Process of preparing illustrations for printing by transferring their images to metal plates by a combination of photography and acid etching.
Genus of large, carnivorous dinosaurs of the suborder Theropoda, that walked on stout hindlegs with large birdlike feet and used their heavy tails for balance.
American astronomer and physicist who devised the meridian photometer with which he made more than 1,400,000 measurements of the magnitudes of stars.
English blacksmith and inventor who developed a steam engine that utilized both atmospheric pressure and low-pressure steam and was widely used for water pumping in most of Europe.
First of the three divisions of the Mesozoic era of the geologic time scale, characterized by the disintegration of the supercontinent Panagea.
British mathematician, physicist, and astronomer who is best known for his successful application of mathematics to problems in physics and astronomy.
Mathematical notation consisting of a square array of numbers between two vertical bars; the value of the expression is determined by its expansion according to certain rules.
A grayish-black granular variety of corundum that contains hematite or magnetite and is used extensively as an abrasive in grinding wheels.
American mineralogist whose most important work was a Manual of Mineralogy, first published in 1848 and reissued with revisions up to the present time.
American physicist who was awarded the 1923 Nobel Prize in physics for his famous 'oil-drop' experiments that measured the charge on an electron.
American inventor who developed a practical electric light bulb, electric generating system, sound-recording device, and motion picture projector.
Minerals composed of hydrated aluminum silicates of alkali metals and alkaline earth metals; they are named for their swelling and bubbling under high temperature.
Group of naturally occurring organic compounds,  lighter than and insoluble in water, comprised of three molecules of fatty acids and one molecule of the alcohol gylcerol.
In relation to computers, a record-keeping arrangement used by the vendor of an online service to identify a subscriber and to maintain a record of customer usage for billing purposes.
American physicist and Nobel laureate who developed a theory involving the unification of weak interactions and electromagnetism.
This white, translucent, lustrous mineral composed of potassium nitrate (KNO3) was once used in gunpowder and is now found in explosives, fireworks, and matches.
In computer graphics, the effect produced when display resolution is too coarse to minimize the jagged appearance of diagonal lines, curves, and circles.
American chemist and Nobel laureate who developed gas-filled tungsten lamps, electron-discharge apparatus, high-vacuum pumps, and the atomic-hydrogen welding torch.
American engineer, inventor, designer, architect, writer, educator, philosopher, and poet, noted for his innovative use of technology.
Russian-American theoretical physicist who made important contributions in radioactivity, cosmogony, astrophysics, and nuclear physics.
French physicist and Nobel laureate who made major contributions to the theory of quantum mechanics with his studies of electromagnetic radiation.
In cosmology, theory developed in the early 1980s by the United States physicist Alan Guth to help account for events in the very first moments of the creation of the universe.
French astronomer and mathematician who is best known for his successful application of Newton's theory of gravitation to account for all planetary motion in the solar system.
American chemist and Nobel laureate noted for his work in the synthesis of such chemicals as quinine, cholesterol, and cortisone.
German-American electrical engineer and inventor who is best known for his research in the field of electricity, particularly on the nature and use of alternating current.
German chemist who, with the German physicist Gustav Robert Kirchhoff, invented the spectroscope and discovered spectrum analysis.
These organic compounds are thermoplastic, impervious to water, and have low densities, making them suitable for the manufacture of  molded structural materials, adhesives, and textile fibers.
Alteration of the speed of a chemical reaction through the presence of an additional substance that remains chemically unchanged by the reaction.
American inventor and electrical engineer who produced a high-intensity arc searchlight that was widely used by armed forces during World War I.
Swedish biochemist and Nobel laureate who was awarded the 1948 Nobel Prize in chemistry for his work on the separation of colloids through electrophoresis.
British astronomer and chemist who made important contributions to the field of photography, including development of the chemical fixing solution called hypo.
British biochemist and molecular biologist; he was a two time Nobel Prize winner for his research on the structure of proteins and the base sequences in nucleic acids.
German-American physicist, chemist, and Nobel laureate whose research provided experimental verification of the quantum theory.
Italian physicist who shared the 1984 Nobel Prize in physics with the Dutch physicist Simon van der Meer for their discovery of the subatomic W and Z particles.
British civil engineer known for the construction of several notable bridges, including the Victoria Bridge at Berwick-upon-Tweed, England.
In mathematics, branch of number theory concerned with determining the solutions in integers of algebraic equations with two or more unknowns.
Dutch physical chemist who was awarded the first Nobel Prize in chemistry for his work relating thermodynamics to chemical reactions and his studies of the properties of solutions.
Phenomenon discovered by Pierre and Jacques Curie in 1880 in which an electric potential appears across certain faces of a crystal when it is subjected to mechanical pressure.
Mechanical device formerly used by engineers and scientists for rapid and approximate multiplication, division, extraction of roots, raising to powers, and other simple computations.
American scientist, who won the 1968 Nobel Prize in physics for developing the liquid hydrogen bubble-chamber, with which he found atomic particles produced by high-energy nuclear events.
English astronomer who was the first Astronomer Royal of England and the first director of the Royal Greenwich Observatory upon its founding in 1675.
In quantum mechanics, theory stating that it is impossible to specify simultaneously the position and momentum of a particle, such as an electron, with precision.
English mathematician and physicist, considered one of the greatest scientists in history, who made important contributions to many fields of science.
From 1970 to 1980, the joint name of Mount Wilson Observatory and Palomar Observatory, two astronomical observatories in southern California.
Volatile, colorless, liquid (CH3CHO) with a pungent, fruity odor; it is an important intermediate compound in the manufacture of many chemicals, pharmaceuticals, and plastics.
Study of the astronomical achievements of ancient peoples, from prehistoric times to the advanced cultures of the Middle East and Latin America.
In chemistry, an analytical technique based on the principle of selective adsorption that is used for the chemical separation of mixtures and substances.
Field of chemistry that studies the chemical reactions and properties of all the chemical elements and their compounds, with the exception of the hydrocarbons and their derivitives.
Second division of the Cenozoic era of the geological time scale; it was originally defined in the 19th century by the British geologist Sir Charles Lyell.
Hungarian mathematician who was one of the founders of non-Euclidean geometry, independently of Nikolay Lobachevsky and Carl Friedrich Gauss.
Dutch physicist and Nobel laureate whose research into the influence of magnetism upon radiation confirmed the electromagnetic theory of light.
In relation to computers, a strategy that allows a database to be restored to the latest complete unit of work (transaction) after a software or hardware error has rendered the database unusable.
This compact, granular mineral, transparent to translucent, is usually composed of hydrated calcium magnesium silicate(Ca2Mg5 Si8O22(OH)2), source of the mineral jade.
Arab astronomer and mathematician who introduced the use of sines in mathematical calculations, computed a table of cotangents, and formulated propositions in spherical trigonometry.
American physicist and Nobel laureate who studied X rays; in 1922 he discovered that high energy electromagnetic radiation changes its wavelength when it scatters off electrons.
Loose, incoherent mass of mineral materials in a finely granular condition, usually consisting of quartz with a small proportion of mica, feldspar, magnetite, and other resistant minerals.
American astronomer, known for his research in the fields of photometry, cosmology, and spectroscopy and his investigations of Cepheid variable stars and of star clusters.
A small computer introduced in 1975 by Micro Instrumentation Telemetry Systems; it is considered to have been the first successful 'personal' computer.
In physics, a theory based on using the concept of the quantum unit to describe the dynamic properties of subatomic particles and the interactions of matter and radiation.

Theory which originally attempted to account for anomalies in the concept of relative motion, but which in its ramifications has developed into one of the most important basic concepts in physical science.
Italian physicist and astronomer, who, with Johannes Kepler, initiated the scientific revolution that flowered in the work of Sir Isaac Newton.
Dutch-American theoretical physicist and Nobel laureate known for his studies of molecular structure, dipole movements, and the diffraction of X rays and electrons in gases.
British theoretical physicist and Nobel laureate renowned for his prediction of the existence of the positron, or antielectron, and for his research in quantum theory.
This British astronomer and Nobel laureate directed the compilation of the Cambridge catalogs of radio sources that provided the basis for the discovery of quasars.

Hungarian-American aeronautical engineer who developed the first theory of supersonic drag, designed supersonic wind tunnels, and initiated research that led to the first breaking of the sound barrier by an aircraft.

American mathematical physicist who is author of a series of papers collectively entitled On the Equilibrium of Heterogeneous Substances, considered one of the greatest achievements in physical science in the 19th century.
Hypothetical flammable substance postulated in the late 17th century by the German chemists Johann Becher and Georg Stahl to explain the phenomenon of combustion.

Third division of the Paleozoic Era of the geologic time scale; it was  named for an early Brittanic tribe, the Silures, who inhabited the region of western England and Wales where sedimentary rocks of this age were first studied.
